name: Swabian Pockets (Maultaschen)
recipeInstructions:
If anyone insists that 'Maultaschen' are the most delicious of all Swabian specialties, I[ÿrst Scharfenberg] would hardly be prepared to deny it. In fact, as indicated earlier, I suspect that 'Maultaschen' would have very good chances in a four-way international competition with ravioli, won tons, and pirogi for the championship of the Roughly Rectangular Pasta with Meat (plus Miscellaneous) Filling division. It has been said that 'Maultaschen' were originally invented to allow Swabians to keep eating meat during Lent by concealing it beneath the pasta shell and amidst the spinach filling from the eye of the parish priest (if not the omniscient Deity Himself). The following recipe is typical but far from definitive, especially where the ingredients for the filling are concerned. Feel free to use whatever you have on hand or whatever your fancy dictates.
Dough: Combine enough beef stock or salted water to cook the 'Maultaschen'. Mix flour, eggs, and salt in a bowl. Add a little water and knead until firm but elastic. To make the filling, melt butter in a skillet and fry bacon with onions until translucent. Combine the bacon mixture with sausage meat. Moisten hard roll in water, press dry, and grind through a meat grinder (better than food mill or processor), along with bacon mixture, cooked spinach, ground meat, smoked farm sausage, leftover roast, etc. Fold eggs, parsley, and seasonings into the filling; mix together. The filling should be very spicy indeed.
On a floured board, roll dough into rectangular sheets (about twice as wide as desired 'Maultaschen'). Place evenly spaced 3-inch dabs of filling along one side of the sheet. Mix egg and canned milk and apply to spaces between, outer edge, and fold line. Fold plain half over to cover filling, press firmly around packets, and use a pastry wheel or knife to separate into 3-inch square or diamond-shaped 'Maultaschen'. Cook thoroughly in beef stock or boiling salted water for about 10-15 minutes, until they float to the surface. Remove with a slotted spoon and drain.
Serving suggestions: Cut onions into half-rings, fry in butter until golden brown, and empty skillet contents over 'Maultaschen' on serving dish. Serve with potato salad or mixed green salad. Swabian Won Ton Soup: Serve a couple of 'Maultaschen' in hearty beef broth, garnished with finely chopped onion. Swabian Fried Won Tons: Allow boiled 'Maultaschen' to cool, cut into strips, and sauté until crisp. Serve with potato salad. Maultaschen Croque Monsieur: Arrange several portions in an ovenproof casserole, cover with boiled ham and cheese slices, and heat until cheese is desired consistency. Serve with green salad.
